critic_raw:
The primary outcome measure — 'pain reduction 30 days post-surgery' — is described as 'highly correlative with recovery speed and gives frequent statistical signals.' This substitution of a correlated proxy for the actual outcome of interest (recovery time, as stated in the experiment title) is methodologically problematic. MAPE is then applied to predict pain reduction rather than recovery time. If the model is evaluated on pain reduction rather than days-to-recovery, the experiment does not test the stated hypothesis. Additionally, pain scores 30 days post-surgery are patient-reported and highly susceptible to subjective bias, Hawthorne effects, and variable follow-up compliance — none of which are addressed.

Second, surgeon ID is included as a feature in both models. Surgeon ID is a proxy for unobserved skill, technique, and case selection behavior — surgeons with better outcomes may select easier cases, creating confounding. More critically, if the surgeon population in the test set differs from training (new surgeons, retirements, changes in surgical team composition), surgeon ID becomes an unstable feature. The design includes it without addressing this distribution shift risk.

Third, preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. For a regression problem where the target is pain reduction, any normalization of input features using test-set statistics biases the scaling toward the test distribution. The design is explicit: 'all transformers are initially fitted on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly.' This is data leakage.

Fourth, the comparison is fundamentally unfair: CNN receives MRI images plus all clinical/demographic/surgical features; XGBoost receives only clinical/demographic/surgical features. The design acknowledges this but frames it as 'whether CNN outperforms XGBoost with structural features alone.' This is not a controlled comparison — any performance difference could be attributed to MRI image information rather than to model architecture. To test whether CNN architecture adds value over the baseline, the comparison should include an XGBoost model trained on extracted image features (e.g., from the same ResNet backbone) in addition to clinical features.

Fifth, the dataset is drawn exclusively from patients aged 60 and above from a single hospital over 24 months. With a 70% training split, approximately 1,400 training cases (assuming 2,000 total — not stated). Fine-tuning ResNet-18 on 1,400 examples for a regression task with a noisy, subjective outcome is likely underpowered, and the design does not provide a sample size justification or power analysis.
defender_raw:
This CNN for post-surgical recovery prediction design is the most thinly specified case in the chunk and has several serious problems that require concession rather than defense.

The outcome measure switch is the most fundamental problem. The hypothesis states the CNN predicts 'post-surgical recovery times' — a continuous outcome reflecting actual recovery duration. Section 1 then redefines the outcome as 'pain reduction 30 days post-surgery' because it 'is highly correlative with recovery speed and gives frequent statistical signals.' This is a proxy substitution without validation: pain reduction at 30 days is not recovery time. A patient could have high pain reduction but still require extended physical therapy, missed work, or reoperation. The design does not establish that pain reduction at 30 days is a valid proxy for the recovery time claimed in the hypothesis, and this is a scope validity failure.

The data collection window (24 months) and population (age ≥60 only) are stated without justification for why these restrictions do not affect generalizability to the stated hypothesis. The age restriction is particularly consequential — knee replacement patients under 60 have different biomechanical profiles, recovery trajectories, and comorbidity patterns.

The preprocessing is fit on the entire dataset before splitting, which is the same leakage error seen throughout this case set.

The MAPE primary metric is mathematically problematic for this domain: if actual recovery times (or the pain reduction proxy) include values near zero, MAPE is undefined or extremely sensitive to small actual values. For continuous outcome prediction in clinical settings, MAE or RMSE with domain-appropriate normalization are more stable choices.

On the positive side: the stratification by operative time and implant type is a genuine strength. Surgical procedure heterogeneity is a real confound for post-operative outcomes, and stratifying the split on these variables ensures the test distribution reflects the training distribution with respect to these known confounders. The inclusion of surgeon ID as a post-hoc confound control is also appropriate — surgeon skill is a known covariate in surgical outcome research.

The ImageNet pretrained ResNet-18 fine-tuned on MRI scans is a reasonable starting architecture. Transfer learning from ImageNet to medical imaging is well-established despite the domain gap, and fine-tuning on the training set is the correct protocol.
all_issues_raised: The experiment title claims to predict 'recovery time' but the actual outcome variable is 'pain reduction at 30 days' — a subjective proxy outcome — making the hypothesis as stated untestable.
Surgeon ID is included as a feature without addressing the risk that surgeon composition changes between train and test periods, creating an unstable, confounded feature.
Preprocessing transformers are explicitly fit on the entire dataset before splitting, leaking test-set statistics into training normalization for both models.
The CNN receives MRI images while XGBoost does not, meaning any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to architecture — it may simply reflect the information content of MRI.

With an unspecified but likely small sample size (~2,000 patients estimated from 24 months), fine-tuning ResNet-18 for a regression task with a noisy subjective outcome is likely underpowered, and no power analysis is provided.
all_issues_adjudicated: The experiment title claims to predict 'recovery time' but the actual outcome variable is 'pain reduction at 30 days' — a subjective proxy outcome — making the hypothesis as stated untestable.
Surgeon ID is included as a feature without addressing the risk that surgeon composition changes between train and test periods, creating an unstable, confounded feature.
Preprocessing transformers are explicitly fit on the entire dataset before splitting, leaking test-set statistics into training normalization for both models.
The CNN receives MRI images while XGBoost does not, meaning any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to architecture — it may simply reflect the information content of MRI.

With an unspecified but likely small sample size (~2,000 patients estimated from 24 months), fine-tuning ResNet-18 for a regression task with a noisy subjective outcome is likely underpowered, and no power analysis is provided.
